You can use **Command (⌘) + Option (⌥) + Tab (⇥)** to switch between different layouts, or add **Shift (⇧)** to move backwards through the list.[4] Cursor includes four default layouts: agent, editor, zen, and browser.[4]<br/><br/>## Customizing and Saving Workspace Layout<br/><br/>**Panel Customization:**<br/>You can click and drag panels to rearrange them, use the View menu to show/hide panels, and adjust panel sizes as needed.[2] The terminal can be moved to the bottom or side with adjustable height/width.[2]<br/><br/>**Saving Layout Preferences:**<br/>Cursor allows you to save workspace layouts and use workspace profiles to preserve your setup.[2] You can also enable built-in Settings Sync to synchronize your UI state (window layout, panel positions) across different machines when signed into your account.[5]<br/><br/>**Layout Configuration Tips:**<br/>To prevent automatic panel maximization and maintain terminal persistence, you can add settings to your `settings.json` file:[3]<br/>```<br/>\{<br/>  "workbench.panel.opensMaximized": false,<br/>  "terminal.integrated.persistentSessionReviveProcess": "never",<br/>  "workbench.layoutControl.enabled": false<br/>\}<br/>```<br/><br/>## Recent Improvements<br/><br/>Version 2.3 of Cursor included significant stability improvements to layout controls and made it easier to customize your default layout across workspaces.[4] |

</details>

---


Here’s how Cursor handles layouts and how to use them for “agent view” vs “coding view”:

## Built-in layout switching

Cursor 2.3+ includes four layouts you can switch between with the keyboard:

- **Agent** – AI/chat prominent  
- **Editor** – code-focused  
- **Zen** – minimal UI  
- **Browser** – browser-style layout  

**Shortcuts:**

- **⌘ + Option + Tab** – next layout  
- **⌘ + Option + Shift + Tab** – previous layout  

So for “primarily agent view” vs “coding view”, use **Agent** and **Editor** and flip between them with those shortcuts.

## Saving and reusing your setup

Cursor doesn’t expose “save this as Layout #5” in the UI. What it does support:

1. **Default layout per workspace**  
   Arrange panels (sidebar, terminal, chat, etc.) how you want, then rely on **Settings Sync** so that UI state (window layout, panel positions) is remembered when you’re signed in.

2. **Workspace profiles**  
   Use different profiles (e.g. “Agent workflow” vs “Coding”) and keep different panel arrangements per profile; Cursor/VS Code will try to keep layout per profile.

3. **Manual panel tuning**  
   - Drag panels to reposition  
   - **View** → show/hide panels (sidebar, terminal, etc.)  
   - Resize splits and panel heights  

   Once you have “agent” and “editor” arrangements you like, **⌘ + Option + Tab** will still flip between the built-in Agent/Editor/Zen/Browser layouts. Your custom panel layout is whatever you last had when you used that layout.

## Practical workflow for agent vs coding

- Use **⌘ + Option + Tab** to switch between **Agent** and **Editor** as your two main layouts.  
- Optionally create two workspace profiles (“Agent”, “Coding”) and set up each profile’s panels the way you want; then switch profiles when you change mode, and use the same shortcuts to change layout within that profile.

So you don’t “save” custom layout names, but you get quick switching between Agent and Editor, and you can refine and persist that via workspace layout + profiles + Settings Sync.

[Layout Customization and Stability Improvements](https://cursor.com/changelog/2-3)<br/>2. [Troubleshooting Guide](https://www.aidoczh.com/cursor/troubleshooting/troubleshooting-guide.html)<br/>3. [The Hidden Terminal Behavior in Cursor IDE: What Insiders Know About Full-Screen Workflow Disruptions](https://dredyson.com/the-hidden-terminal-behavior-in-cursor-ide-what-insiders-know-about-full-screen-workflow-disruptions/)<br/>4. [Cursor vs. VSCode UI Clutter: I Tested 6 Fixes for Persistent Dialogs (Mac & Windows Results)](https://dredyson.com/cursor-vs-vscode-ui-clutter-i-tested-6-fixes-for-persistent-dialogs-mac-windows-results/)<br/>5. [Fix Agent Sidebar Pop-Ups in Cursor IDE: A Beginner’s Step-by-Step Guide to Avoiding 5 Critical Mistakes](https://dredyson.com/fix-agent-sidebar-pop-ups-in-cursor-ide-a-beginners-step-by-step-guide-to-avoiding-5-critical-mistakes/)<br/><br/># Cursor IDE Layout Not Saving/Persisting<br/><br/>## Layout Customization Features<br/>Cursor version 2.3 introduced improved layout customization with easier default layout management across workspaces.[1] Four default layouts are available (agent, editor, zen, and browser), and you can switch between them using Command (⌘) + Option (⌥) + Tab (⇥).[1]<br/><br/>## Common Layout Persistence Issues<br/><br/>**Layout Reset After Operations:**<br/>Your workspace layout may reset unexpectedly due to Cursor's layout engine behavior. When maximizing panels or opening files, the system can switch layout profiles unintentionally, causing your carefully arranged workspace to reset.[3][4]<br/><br/>**Terminal and Panel Disappearance:**<br/>Terminals and panels may disappear or become hidden after file operations due to how Cursor remembers workspace layout.[3] Phantom panels can persist even after closing.[4]<br/><br/>## Solutions to Fix Layout Not Persisting<br/><br/>**Add these settings to your settings.json:**[3]<br/>```<br/>\{<br/>  "workbench.panel.opensMaximized": false,<br/>  "terminal.integrated.persistentSessionReviveProcess": "never",<br/>  "workbench.layoutControl.enabled": false<br/>\}<br/>```<br/><br/>**Reset App Data (Nuclear Option):**<br/>If layout issues persist, clear all app data:[2]<br/>- **Windows:** Run deletion commands in Command Prompt for AppData folders<br/>- **macOS:** Run `sudo rm -rf ~/Library/Application\ Support/Cursor` in Terminal<br/>- **Linux:** Run `rm -rf ~/.cursor ~/.config/Cursor/` in Terminal<br/><br/>This will reset your entire workspace but fixes persistent configuration problems.[2] |

</details>
</tool-use>

[Allow "~/Library/Application Support/Code/User/workspaceStorage/<someID>/state.vscdb" to be stored on remote server · Issue #179882 · microsoft/vscode](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/179882)<br/>2. [Where is the editor layout stored in VSCode?](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/64466101/where-is-the-editor-layout-stored-in-vscode)<br/>3. [vscode: Where stores the session data (restored opened files)? Is that possible to use relative path?](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/54490827/vscode-where-stores-the-session-data-restored-opened-files-is-that-possible)<br/>4. [What is a VS Code workspace?](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/editing/workspaces/workspaces)<br/>5. [Where does VSCode store the enabled/disabled state for extensions?](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/69706490/where-does-vscode-store-the-enabled-disabled-state-for-extensions)<br/><br/># VS Code Workbench Layout and State Storage<br/><br/>VS Code persists workbench layout and workspace state in the `workspaceStorage` directory, which contains workspace-specific data stored in SQLite format.<br/><br/>## Storage Location<br/><br/>Workspace state is stored in `%APPDATA%\Code\User\workspaceStorage\` on Windows (or equivalent paths on other operating systems).[2][3] Within this directory, each workspace has a hashed folder containing a `state.vscdb` file—a SQLite Format 3 database that stores the workspace state.[2]<br/><br/>## What Gets Stored<br/><br/>The `state.vscdb` file in each workspace stores:[1]<br/>- List of opened files<br/>- Window layout and editor groups<br/>- UI state and other workspace-specific configurations<br/>- Extension enabled/disabled states (stored under `extensionsIdentifiers/disabled` key)[5]<br/><br/>Additionally, there is a `workspace.json` file in each folder with metadata about the workspace path.[3]<br/><br/>## How It Works<br/><br/>When you open a folder in VS Code, the application automatically tracks configuration like open files and editor layout.[4] When you reopen that folder, VS Code restores the exact state it was previously in by reading from the `state.vscdb` file.[2][4]<br/><br/>**Note:** The state storage is workspace-specific and uses absolute paths, so moving or renaming a workspace folder will cause the session data to be lost since VS Code cannot find the workspace using its stored identifier.[3] |

</details>
</tool-use>

  "markdown": "Cursor vs. VSCode UI Clutter: I Tested 6 Fixes for Persistent Dialogs (Mac &amp;amp; Windows Results) - Dre Dyson\n# [Dre Dyson](https://dredyson.com)\n[Resume](https://dredyson.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/DDyson_ResumeNov2025.pdf)\n* [LinkedIn](https://linkedin.com/in/ergosumdre)\n* [Email](mailto:dre@dredyson)\nSearch posts and pages\n![Cursor](https://dredyson.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Cursor-AI-1-219x146.png)\n# Cursor vs. VSCode UI Clutter: I Tested 6 Fixes for Persistent Dialogs (Mac &amp;#038; Windows Results)\nOctober 24, 2025\n## I Tried Fixing Cursor’s Cluttered UI –Here’s How It Stacks Up Against VSCode\nFor two weeks, I wrestled with Cursor’s persistent dialogs and unresponsive panels &amp;#8211; the kind that make you miss VSCode’s clean workspace. I tested six popular fixes across both Mac and Windows to see which solutions stick. Let’s cut through the noise and compare what actually works.\n## Cursor vs VSCode: When Helpful Features Become Noise\nWhile VSCode stays quietly efficient, Cursor’s AI helpers can feel like overeager coworkers. Through 48 hours of testing, three issues kept resurfacing:\n* **Chat Panel Freeze:**That left-side panel that forgets its close button (especially on Mac)\n* **Suggestion Spam:**AI dialog boxes that won’t take &amp;#8220;no&amp;#8221; for an answer\n* **Memory Loss:**Your carefully arranged workspace resetting after lunch## My Testing Setup: Real Machines, Real Annoyances\nI ran trials on both platforms many developers use daily:\n* MacBook Pro (M1, Ventura)\n* Windows 11 desktop (Ryzen 7)\nFor each fix, I tracked three key things:\n* How long setup took\n* Whether it survived a coffee break\n* What features broke in the process## 6 Fixes Put to the Test: What Worked, What Crashed\n### 1. Layout Toggle Workaround (58% Success)\n**The Play:**Flipping between layouts like a short-order cook\n&gt; Settings &gt; Interface &gt; Toggle Layout Mode\n**Wins:**\n* Sometimes clears panels instantly\n* No scary terminal commands\n**Fails:**\n* Mac users: bring patience (3-5 tries average)\n* Says goodbye to your custom workspace### 2. Settings Reset (100% Success)\n**The Play:**Nuclear option via command line:\n&#96;rm -rf \\~/Library/Application\\\\ Support/Cursor/User&#96;\n**Wins:**\n* Guaranteed fresh start\n* Fixed weird subscription bugs too\n**Fails:**\n* You’ll need to redo your preferences\n* Windows folks: watch those file paths### 3. Panel Positioning (31% Success)\n**The Play:**Right-click/Ctrl-click panel headers\n**Reality Check:**Worked when it felt like it &amp;#8211; context menus ghosted me 69% of the time, especially on left-aligned panels.\n### 4. Tab Closing (89% Win / 12% Mac)\n**The Play:**Clicking those tiny X’s on chat tabs\n**Surprise:**Windows handled it smoothly, but my Mac left phantom panels haunting my workspace.\n### 5. Downgrading to v1.8.3\n**Shocker:**Older version had cleaner UI but:\n* New AI models wouldn’t play nice\n* Git features went missing### 6. The VSCode Sidecar Strategy\n**The Play:**Use VSCode for editing, Cursor just for AI\n**Tradeoff:**Adds seconds per switch but saves minutes in frustration.\n## Mac vs Windows: The Great UI Divide\nThe numbers don’t lie &amp;#8211; platform matters:\n* **Missing Close Buttons:**83% Mac vs 11% Windows\n* **Layout Toggle Success:**94% Windows vs 35% Mac\n* **Shortcut Confusion:**Mac’s Cmd/Ctrl mashups vs Windows’ consistency## What These Fixes Cost Your Workday\nTracking two weeks of work revealed:\n|Solution|Implementation Time|Mental Reboot Time|\nLayout Toggle|47s|2.3m|\nFull Reset|3.2m|6.1m|\nVSCode Switch|8s|0s|\n## What I Actually Recommend\nAfter 142 attempts, here’s my straight talk:\n* **Windows Users:**Try closing tabs first, reset only when desperate\n* **Mac Team:**Keep that config deletion command handy &amp;#8211; you’ll need it\n* **Teams Considering Cursor:**Wait for v2.2 before serious adoption## The Final Tally: Cursor vs VSCode\nCursor’s AI impresses, but its interface often fights you. Until updates stabilize things, here’s my survival plan:\n1. Keep VSCode as your primary editor\n2. Fire up Cursor only for AI-heavy tasks\n3. Practice that config deletion command\nA tool should help you code, not become another problem to solve. Sometimes the best fix is keeping VSCode just a click away.\n## Related Resources\nYou might also find these related articles helpful:\n* **[Cursor IDE for Beginners: How to Master the Interface and Avoid Common Frustrations](https://dredyson.com/cursor-ide-for-beginners-how-to-master-the-interface-and-avoid-common-frustrations/)**&amp;#8211; Getting Started with Cursor IDE: Your First Steps Without the Headaches If you&amp;#8217;re just starting with Cursor &amp;#038;#8211&amp;#8230;\n* **[Cursor&amp;#8217;s UI Crisis: A Technical Postmortem of How Good Intentions Created the New Clippy](https://dredyson.com/cursors-ui-crisis-a-technical-postmortem-of-how-good-intentions-created-the-new-clippy/)**&amp;#8211; What surprised me most during my technical analysis of Cursor&amp;#8217;s interface issues When I first noticed Cursor&amp;#038;#8217&amp;#8230;\n* **[How I Fixed the Annoying Uncloseable Cursor Chat Panel (Mac &amp;#038; Windows Guide)](https://dredyson.com/how-i-fixed-the-annoying-uncloseable-cursor-chat-panel-mac-windows-guide/)**&amp;#8211; I Was Ready to Throw My Coffee at the Screen Let&amp;#8217;s be real &amp;#8211; we&amp;#8217;ve all been there. I switched to Curso&amp;#8230;\nPosted in:[Cursor](https://dredyson.com/category/cursor/)\nTags:[AI](https://dredyson.com/tag/ai/),[Comparison Study](https://dredyson.com/tag/comparison-study/),[cursor](https://dredyson.com/tag/cursor/),[Development](https://dredyson.com/tag/development/)\n![Ads Blocker Image Powered by Code Help Pro](https://dredyson.com/wp-content/plugins/chp-ads-block-detector/assets/img/icon.png)\n#### Ads Blocker Detected!!!\nWe have detected that you are using extensions to block ads. [Syncing settings across different machines](https://cursor.fan/tutorial/HowTo/syncing-settings-across-different-machines/)<br/>4. [Settings \| Cursor Documentation](https://cursorcommunity.com/docs/settings)<br/>5. [The Hidden Terminal Behavior in Cursor IDE: What Insiders Know About Full-Screen Workflow Disruptions](https://dredyson.com/the-hidden-terminal-behavior-in-cursor-ide-what-insiders-know-about-full-screen-workflow-disruptions/)<br/><br/># Cursor Layout Configuration in settings.json<br/><br/>## Saving Layout Configuration<br/><br/>Cursor stores layout settings in `settings.json`, which can be accessed and modified to customize your workspace layout.[2][3]<br/><br/>### How to Access and Modify Layout Settings<br/><br/>1. Open the command palette with `Ctrl+Shift+P`<br/>2. Select **Preferences: Open Settings (JSON)**<br/>3. Add layout configuration lines to your settings file[2]<br/><br/>### Key Layout Settings<br/><br/>The primary settings for controlling layout orientation are:[2]<br/><br/>```json<br/>\{<br/>  "workbench.activityBar.orientation": "vertical",<br/>  "workbench.activityBar.location": "side",<br/>  "update.mode": "default"<br/>\}<br/>```<br/><br/>### Additional Layout Control Settings<br/><br/>To prevent automatic panel maximization and preserve terminal states, add:[5]<br/><br/>```json<br/>\{<br/>  "workbench.panel.opensMaximized": false,<br/>  "terminal.integrated.persistentSessionReviveProcess": "never",<br/>  "workbench.layoutControl.enabled": false<br/>\}<br/>```<br/><br/>### Saving Your Layout<br/><br/>After modifying settings.json, **fully restart Cursor** for changes to take effect—live refresh is not available for layout changes.[2] Settings are automatically saved when you modify the JSON file.[3]<br/><br/>### Settings Storage Location<br/><br/>Your `settings.json` file is stored in:[3]<br/>- **Windows**: `%APPDATA%\Cursor\User\`<br/>- **macOS**: `~/Library/Application Support/Cursor/User/`<br/>- **Linux**: `~/.config/Cursor/User/` |

</details>
</tool-use>
